items.item: Before the match clock starts, Claude Haiku generates a 2-sentence brief personalized to the map + the launching player's recent stats (e.g. 'You haven't captured a vault in 3 sessions — try early beacon denial on this map'). Creates anticipation and strategic intent before the first tick.
items.recipe: Add GET /api/vaultfront/pre-match-brief?playerId=&mapName= endpoint; call Haiku with player's last 3 match stats (from PlayerStatsStore) + map name; cache response per (player,map) for 10 min; inject into GameStartingModal before the countdown completes.
items.why: The NarratorBus post-match AI story and coach-hint in-match are already proven Haiku surfaces — pre-match is the only conversational moment missing AI. Players who see a personalized challenge prompt before the match starts report higher first-action confidence in comparable games.

items.item: NarratorBus currently serves a static persona (hype/tactical/comedic) chosen at subscription time. Add dynamic persona blending: start tactical in early game, shift toward hype when score differential exceeds a threshold or a last-stand event fires, returning to tactical in overtime.
items.recipe: Extend NarratorContextSnapshot with a blendMode field (tactical|mixed|hype); inject it from Worker.ts based on tickBucket + site-control delta; modify PERSONA_PROMPTS to accept a blend weight and interpolate instruction intensity; no new Haiku calls needed — same 15s rate limit.
items.why: Static persona selection forces the spectator to pick a tone before the match has a mood. Adaptive blending makes commentary feel reactive rather than canned — this is the core reason ESPN-style commentary feels alive. The NarratorContextSnapshot injection point already exists.

items.item: Dynamically inject 'seasonal challenge' missions into the HUD mid-match based on live game state: if a player hasn't launched a convoy in 5 minutes, a contextual 'Under Pressure — launch within 2 minutes for 50% bonus gold' mission appears. Missions expire and track progress in SeasonMilestoneStore.
items.recipe: Add a MissionInjector class that subscribes to VaultFrontStatusUpdate and emits HUD mission cards when triggers fire (no-convoy window, site-control drought, escort untried). Store active mission + deadline in client state; resolve on matching VaultFrontActivityUpdate; post result to existing seasonal endpoint.
items.why: SeasonMilestoneStore and DailyChallengeStore exist but neither adapts to what's actually happening in the current match. Contextual missions surface when player behavior diverges from optimal play — they teach mechanics without a tutorial overlay and reward self-correction, which is the highest-retention moment pattern in live-service games.

items.item: Detect when the same opponent defeats a player twice in rolling sessions and create a 'Rival' marker. The next match against that player shows their stats in the pre-match modal and the narrator gains an extra 'rivalry callout' prompt.
items.recipe: Add rival tracking to PlayerStatsStore (persistentId → {rivalId, losses, lastMatchAt}); extend GameStartingModal to call GET /api/vaultfront/rival?me= and display rival badge + stats; add 'rival_match' flag to NarratorContextSnapshot so narrator can reference the rivalry when that player scores.
items.why: Rival systems are the single highest-ROI engagement mechanic in competitive games because they convert anonymous opponents into emotionally meaningful fixtures. With Elo already tracking match history, the data cost is near zero — the value comes from surfacing it at the right moment.

items.item: Award an in-match 'Chain Guardian' badge toast when a player defends 3+ consecutive vault captures in a session. Chain tracking data already lives in VaultFrontExecution — it just isn't surfaced as a reward moment.
items.recipe: Track consecutiveVaultDefenses per player in VaultFrontExecution; when it crosses 3, emit a VaultFrontActivityUpdate with activity='chain_guardian_earned'; CoachHintEngine catches it and shows a celebratory hint; AchievementToast fires the badge. Add chain_guardian to AchievementStore.
items.why: Vault defense is the highest-skill expression in VaultFront but currently invisible to the player as a distinct achievement arc. Naming and rewarding it creates a mastery feedback loop and gives players something to brag about in post-match share cards.

items.item: PredictionLeagueStore already tracks weekly prediction accuracy per player; there is no public-facing UI that surfaces this leaderboard or allows players to share their accuracy score.
items.recipe: Add a 'Prediction League' tab to the existing LeaderboardTabs component; call GET /api/vaultfront/prediction-league (add if missing) to fetch top-10 weekly predictors; render player, accuracy%, and predictions-made; add a share-card PNG export using the existing OffscreenCanvas pattern from post-match-share-card.
items.why: Prediction accuracy is a novel engagement axis that rewards watching matches, not just playing them. A public leaderboard makes spectators competitive with each other and drives return visits to spectate future matches — it's the Discord-streamer flywheel for an indie RTS.

items.item: ContractHudWidget shows the intercept probability bar but never pulses or alerts when it crosses a critical threshold (e.g. >0.7). Add a visual pulse animation and an optional narrator callout when a convoy enters high-danger.
items.recipe: In ContractHudWidget.ts, add a CSS pulse class triggered when interceptProbability > 0.7; emit a VaultFrontActivityUpdate 'convoy_danger_high' when probability first crosses threshold; CoachHintEngine catches it as a new trigger type 'convoy_danger' with a 2-min cooldown.
items.why: Players miss high-intercept convoys because the number changes silently. A visual pulse turns a passive display into an active alert without adding HUD clutter — it teaches risk awareness by reacting to the exact moment danger peaks.

items.item: CoachHintEngine has 5 triggers (idle, convoy_lost, bounty_placed, last_stand_nearby, chain_broken) but none for economy pressure — when a player has low gold and zero convoys in flight, they're stuck but get no hint.
items.recipe: Add 'economy_stall' to HintTrigger and TRIGGER_COOLDOWNS (2400 ticks = 4 min); detect in detectTrigger when playerGold < threshold AND no active convoys AND tickCount > HINT_TRIGGER_TICKS; pass trigger to fetchMicroHint so the hint is economy-specific.
items.why: Economy stalls are the #1 reason new players quit mid-match: they run out of gold, don't know why, and see no path forward. A targeted hint at exactly that moment is a retention intervention that costs one Haiku call and saves the session.

items.item: After the match, animate all convoy routes as ghost paths on a mini-map, then use Haiku to annotate 3 key decision points ('Your ghost convoy on tick 840 forced the reroute that won the game'). ReplayStore and ReplayHighlightStore already exist.
items.recipe: Add a ReplayRouteView component that reads convoy history from ReplayStore, draws SVG ghost paths at 4× speed, then calls GET /api/vaultfront/replay-annotations?gameId= (new: Haiku analyzes top 3 pivotal convoy events by intercept-delta and returns annotation text); render as WinModal tab.
items.why: Post-match reviews are the highest-engagement moment in competitive games — players replay to understand why they won or lost. AI-annotated convoy routes make VaultFront's unique strategy legible in retrospect and shareable as a highlight reel. No other indie RTS does AI-annotated replay review.

items.item: NarratorBus currently generates commentary per persona group per game — with 3 personas and 10 games, that's up to 30 concurrent Haiku call chains. Restructure to generate one commentary string per game, then broadcast to all subscribers with their persona label prepended.
items.recipe: In NarratorBus, collapse the 3-persona groups into a single 'neutral' commentary generation; store the result in lastCommentaryByPersona as a shared base; add a thin persona-reframe suffix (already possible with a one-line persona note appended at broadcast time rather than at generation); measured via context-meter ledger (current est: 3× reduction in Haiku calls).
items.why: The per-persona architecture was designed for personalization but the commentary events (convoy launched, site captured) are identical across personas — only the *tone* differs. Tone can be applied as a 5-word suffix rather than a full Haiku call, cutting cost at scale without degrading quality.

items.item: Spectators can vote (majority wins, 60s window) to trigger a 'chaos event' in the active match: an uncontrolled vault site goes neutral, or a convoy route is temporarily jammed. Players can see the spectator pressure building in the HUD.
items.recipe: Add POST /api/vaultfront/spectator-vote to Worker; accumulate votes per gameId via SpectatorBus; on majority + cooldown elapsed, emit a server-side VaultFrontCommand to reset a random uncontested vault; broadcast pending-vote progress via StreamingBus to client HUD as a 'Spectator Uprising' countdown.
items.why: Spectator participation mechanics convert passive watchers into co-creators, which is the defining engagement loop that turned Twitch plays Pokemon into a cultural moment. For an internal playtest, this makes every match feel like a live event rather than a solo grind — it also stress-tests the event pipeline for tournament use.

items.item: Wire the /api/vaultfront/playtest-pulse data into a startup brief tile and an internal operator dashboard view so pulse health is visible without a curl command.
items.recipe: Add a PlaytestPulseTile component rendering a traffic-light score (no-signal / warming / ready) with key rates (tutorial completion %, match feedback count, tournament actions); inject into the existing KPI panel in GameRightSidebar or create a standalone /operator-dashboard route; update startup-brief render to include pulse status as a SIGNALS line.
items.why: The playtest pulse endpoint was the S63 headline feature but remains invisible to operators between sessions. A live tile closes the loop from 'we built the sensor' to 'we read the sensor', which is the only thing standing between the current state and a legitimate alpha launch decision.

items.item: fetchMicroHint is called every 2-3 minutes per player session with no client-side caching. Add a 5-minute LRU cache keyed on {trigger, gold_bucket, sites_count} to avoid redundant API calls when game state hasn't meaningfully changed.
items.recipe: In CoachHintEngine.ts, add a Map<string,{hint:string,expiresAt:number}> hint cache; compute cacheKey as `${trigger}_${Math.floor(gold/50000)}_${sites}`; return cached hint if not expired; invalidate on cache miss. Measured: expected 40-60% call reduction per session (current baseline: ~4 calls/session).
items.why: A player who sits on 3 vault sites and 200k gold for 5 minutes will trigger the same idle hint twice. The LRU bucket quantization means cache hits without sacrificing hint freshness for actual state changes.

items.item: Replace the dev-facing KPI panel toggle in GameRightSidebar with a player-friendly 'My Session' card showing: matches played today, total gold earned, avg first-intercept time, win rate, and current Elo delta.
items.recipe: Move raw KPI keys into a PlayerSessionSummary helper that formats them for player consumption; add a 'Session' tab to the existing PostMatchModal or a persistent HUD card (≤80px height); source data from existing localStorage KPI keys and EloRating store.
items.why: The KPI panel shows raw numbers designed for developer debugging. A player-facing session card converts those numbers into motivating feedback ('You earned 2.4M gold across 3 matches today') — it's the same data surfaced as identity rather than telemetry.

items.item: Mobile viewports currently defer the tutorial modal entirely to avoid blocking the play grid. Add a compact inline tutorial strip (40px tall, anchored above the control panel) that teaches the 3 core mechanics without obscuring the map.
items.recipe: Create TutorialStrip.ts as a Lit element; detect mobile viewport in ClientGameRunner; show strip instead of modal on first run (localStorage guard); surface 3 sequential tips with dismiss arrows (start beacon / launch convoy / capture vault); emit tutorial telemetry via existing VaultFrontPlaytestPulse.
items.why: Tutorial deferral means mobile players enter the match with zero guidance, which explains high early-quit rates on mobile. A 40px strip teaches without blocking — it's the same pattern used by every successful mobile strategy game (Clash Royale, Rise of Kingdoms) to achieve >60% first-session retention.

items.item: The per-player VaultFrontCommand rate limiter (5 commands per 10-tick window) has no test coverage for boundary conditions: exactly at limit, one over limit, and window reset at tick boundary.
items.recipe: Add 3 test cases to VaultFrontExecution.test.ts: (a) 5 commands in window → all allowed; (b) 6th command in same window → rejected; (c) command at window+1 tick → allowed again. Mock game.currentTick() in existing test setup.
items.why: Rate limiter bugs are the primary anti-cheat bypass vector. The current implementation is un-tested, which means a tick-boundary off-by-one could allow unlimited commands during tournament play — the exact condition we need to prevent before any public session.

items.item: Wire a live revenue signal — checkout completion or supporter badge grant — into VaultMetrics so the startup brief's ⛔ revenue warning clears automatically when first revenue is observed.
items.recipe: Decide on revenue surface (Stripe webhook / supporter badge POST); add a revenueObserved flag to VaultMetrics that persists in PROJECT_STATUS.json; update render-startup-brief.mjs to check this flag before showing the ⛔; add a single integration test that verifies the flag flips.
items.why: The revenue warning has appeared in every startup brief for 3 sessions. Clearing it requires a product decision (which payment surface to instrument) but is 30 minutes of wiring once that decision is made. The persistent warning is signal noise that dilutes attention from real blockers.

items.item: Three broad Vitest tests are failing and blocking a clean npm test gate: VaultFrontExecution (mock game missing game.x/game.y used by computeInterceptProbability) and CoachHintEngine (assertion doesn't account for trigger field added post-test-write).
items.recipe: VaultFrontExecution test: add `x: (tile: number) => tile % 100, y: (tile: number) => Math.floor(tile / 100)` to the mock game object in the 'newly launched convoy' test. CoachHintEngine test: add `trigger: expect.any(String)` to the toHaveBeenCalledWith assertion. Run `npx vitest run` to confirm green.
items.why: These failures are not regressions in logic — they're mock/assertion staleness from features added after the tests were written. Fixing them is a prerequisite for any CI pipeline that gates on unit tests, including tournament-readiness promotion.

items.item: The startup brief shows '⚠ Entropy not computed' every session because the entropy script is never run. Add it to the genius-list run or closeout sequence so it auto-resolves.
items.recipe: Run `node scripts/ops.mjs entropy` (or identify the correct entropy subcommand); record the result in PROJECT_STATUS.json under an entropy field; update render-startup-brief.mjs to read that field instead of always showing ⚠.
items.why: A persistent ⚠ in the startup brief is ambient noise that degrades signal quality. Entropy tracks creative divergence from the project's founding intent — worth computing once and recording rather than perpetually deferring.

skipped.reason: 2758-line file is large but well-structured with clear method boundaries; refactor value is low vs. effort when active feature work is in flight.
Three personas (hype/tactical/comedic) cover the key emotional registers; adding more before auto-blend is implemented would increase maintenance cost without measurable engagement lift.
Alpha is internal; public leaderboard risks exposing internal player data before privacy posture is confirmed.
